title: Red Wine on Carpet
stainChemistry: Classification: Tannin (polyphenolic). Red wine contains anthocyanin pigments (water-soluble tannins) and proanthocyanidins that bond chemically to carpet fibers through hydrogen bonding. The acidic environment (pH 3–4) enhances staining. Heat or agitation during initial contact drives the tannin deeper into the fiber matrix, making removal exponentially harder. Tannin pigments bond to fibers through hydrogen bonding and van der Waals forces. Over time, tannin oxidizes and polymerizes, shifting from bright (reversible) to brown (resistant). Treatment: acidic tannin formula to break initial hydrogen bonds, followed by oxidizing bleach to cleave chromophore conjugation.
whyThisWorks: Red wine soaks through synthetic carpet into the padding below. Surface cleaning alone won't reach it — the stain hides underneath and wicks back up as the material dries. Tannin (acidic) breaks the hydrogen bonds between pigment and carpet fiber. H₂O₂ 6% cleaves the chromophore double bonds. NSD and acetic acid flush and neutralize. Dwell time matters more than scrubbing force — the chemistry needs time to reach the depth the stain reached.
defaultAssumption: Treat as unknown age / possibly dried.

steps: 1. Apply Tannin (acidic tannin spotter) and tamp gently. Dwell 5–10 minutes. This acidic spotter targets the bonds that wine, coffee, tea, and fruit juice pigments form with carpet fiber. Work from edge toward center.

2. Apply H₂O₂ 6% (professional-grade oxidizer) to the stain. Dwell 5–15 minutes, checking every 5 minutes. This oxidizer breaks down the double bonds that make tannin stains brown. Test an inconspicuous area of carpet first.
3. Flush with NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) with a mild leveling agent. Dwell 2–3 minutes. Removes broken-down tannin pigment and excess oxidizer. Rinse thoroughly.
4. Apply 28% Acetic acid (diluted 1:10) to stop oxidizer activity and restore the carpet fiber. Dwell 1–2 minutes. Blot dry and allow to air dry completely.
products: NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) with leveling agent
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold to ambient — never use hot water immediately on tannin stains in carpet. Heat drives tannin deeper into fiber matrix and accelerates polymerization.
warnings: Using hot water immediately — heat sets tannins permanently into the fiber.
Rubbing or scrubbing instead of blotting — spreads the stain and drives liquid deeper into the pad.
Waiting too long before treating — wine sets chemically within 10–15 minutes on synthetic carpet.
Skipping the acetic acid neutralization — acidic residue can cause yellowing over time.
Test all agents on an inconspicuous area first — monitor for color change, surface dulling, or texture damage.

homeSolutions.agent: Patch test on a hidden spot
homeSolutions.instruction: Find a hidden patch of the same carpet — inside a closet, under a couch, behind a door. Dab a drop of the Dawn + 3% peroxide solution from step 5 and blot with a white cloth after two minutes. If the carpet holds its color, you're safe. If dye transfers to the cloth or the fiber lightens, stop — home peroxide will bleach the carpet. Skip to step 9.

homeSolutions.agent: The tannin rule: no alkaline cleaners
homeSolutions.instruction: Red wine is a tannin, the same family as coffee, tea, and berries. Alkaline products — ammonia, baking soda, OxiClean, most "all-purpose" carpet sprays — lock the tannin into the fiber permanently and turn a purple ring into a dark brown one you can't undo. Use only: cold water, Dawn dish soap, 3% hydrogen peroxide, and white vinegar. Salt and club soda don't hurt, but they don't help either.

Press a clean white cloth or stack of paper towels straight down on the spill with firm pressure. Lift, move to a dry section, press again. Work from the outside of the stain inward so you don't spread it. Keep blotting until the cloth stops picking up red. Rubbing drives wine deep into the pile and the pad below — and once it reaches the pad, it will wick back up even after you clean the surface.

Pour a small amount of cold water directly on the stain — a few tablespoons at most, not a cup — and blot immediately. Repeat two or three times. The goal is to push the wine off the fiber and into your cloth without flooding the pad. Avoid hot water. Heat sets red wine tannin permanently and causes the stain to polymerize to a brown color you can't reverse.

Mix one tablespoon of clear Dawn dish soap with one cup of 3% hydrogen peroxide in a small bowl. Dip a white cloth, wring nearly dry, and dab the stain — outside edges toward center. Let it sit 5–10 minutes, then blot with a clean damp cloth. Peroxide oxidizes the anthocyanin pigment; Dawn lifts it. Most wine stains need 2–3 cycles. This is the workhorse step — give it time.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix hydrogen peroxide with ammonia or bleach — dangerous gases. And skip this step on dark or patterned carpet unless your patch test came back clean.

homeSolutions.instruction: Mix one tablespoon of white vinegar into one cup of cold water. Dab onto the treated spot with a clean white cloth. Mild acid does two things: neutralizes any peroxide residue, and keeps tannin in its red (lighter) form rather than letting it drift toward brown as it dries. Blot dry with a clean cloth.

homeSolutions.agent: Dry down — prevent pad wicking overnight
homeSolutions.instruction: Blot until no more moisture transfers to a dry cloth. Stack eight to twelve sheets of white paper towels over the area, weigh them down with a heavy book, and leave overnight. Wine on carpet is a pad problem — any pigment still in the padding will wick back up as a ring if you don't pull it out while the carpet is still wet. Don't walk on the area or vacuum until completely dry.

homeSolutions.agent: Check under daylight — repeat if shadow remains
homeSolutions.instruction: Once dry, inspect in natural light (not a lamp). Wine rings often look gone when wet but reappear as a pink or tan halo when dry. If you see one, repeat steps 5–7 a second time. Two full rounds is the home ceiling. If a shadow still shows, or if you see a brown (not pink) halo, the tannin has polymerized — escalate.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate to a professional carpet cleaner
homeSolutions.instruction: If the patch test fails, the carpet is wool, or a stain survives two full home rounds, call a pro. Tell them: "Red wine on [nylon/polyester/wool] carpet, treated with Dawn + 3% peroxide, vinegar rinse. No ammonia or alkaline products used. Residual [pink/brown] halo remains." That handoff lets them go straight to a commercial tannin formula, 6% peroxide, and hot-water extraction — the full depth tools you can't run at home.

steps: 1. Blot the stain with a clean white cloth to soak up as much as you can. Getting excess out early makes the rest easier.
2. Mix 1 tbsp dish soap with 1 cup 3% hydrogen peroxide (the brown bottle from the pharmacy). Apply the solution and let it sit for 5–10 minutes.
3. Blot with a clean white cloth. Repeat until the color fades.
4. Blot with a cloth dampened with plain water to rinse — remove all cleaning solution.
5. Place a stack of paper towels over the spot and weigh them down. Let it dry completely. This prevents the stain from wicking back up from the carpet pad.
products: OxiClean Versatile Stain Remover (for set stains)
Wine Away Red Wine Stain Remover
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Apply H₂O₂ (3%) directly and let it dwell for 30-60 minutes. Apply and let it soak for 30–60 minutes. If the stain survives two rounds of treatment, a professional cleaner can use concentrated agents and specialized equipment that outperform any home method.
warnings: Always blot — never rub. Rubbing damages carpet fibers and spreads the stain.
Place paper towels over the wet spot and weigh them down to prevent wicking overnight.
Never use hot water on tannin stains in carpet — heat permanently sets the color.
Test any cleaning solution on an inconspicuous area of carpet first.
Avoid over-wetting — too much liquid soaks through to the pad and causes wicking.

neverDo: Never use hot water — heat sets tannins permanently into carpet fiber.
Never rub or scrub — spreads the stain and drives it deeper.
Never use ammonia or alkaline agents — permanently darkens tannin stains.
Never mix ammonia with chlorine bleach — produces toxic chloramine gas.
Never over-wet the carpet — moisture soaks into pad and causes wicking and delamination.
fiberSensitivities: Synthetic carpet (nylon/polyester) tolerates most agents — test all agents in an inconspicuous area first.
Monitor for color change, surface dulling, or texture damage during treatment.
Natural fiber carpet (wool) requires the wool-safe tannin protocol — different chemistry applies.

escalation.when: If wine stain persists after the full protocol, the tannins have cross-linked into the carpet fiber structure. Next option: rent a hot-water extraction machine (Rug Doctor or similar) and use the tannin remover additive pack. If the stain is larger than 12 inches or on a delicate natural fiber carpet, call a professional carpet cleaner who has industrial-grade tannin removers and hot-water extraction equipment.
escalation.whatToTell: We've treated the red wine on your carpet. Wine darkens with air exposure and sets deeper with time and heat.

contextQuestions: How old is the stain — was it treated immediately or has it been sitting?
Was salt, club soda, or any product applied at time of spill?
Is this natural fiber carpet (wool) or synthetic (nylon/polyester)?
How large is the stain and does it appear to have soaked through to the pad?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Fresh red wine on synthetic carpet responds best — the sooner the treatment, the higher the success rate. Salt or club soda at spill time is neutral. Natural fiber carpet (wool) requires a completely different protocol — no enzymes, no oxidizing peroxide, use sodium hydrosulfite instead. Stains that have soaked into the pad will wick back up after surface treatment and require extraction equipment.
